definition: The most probable outcome of fiscal dominance for advanced economies is prolonged moderate inflation (3-6%) and financial repression producing persistently negative real bond returns — NOT crisis or hyperinflation. This 'boring middle scenario' generates the most significant wealth transfer from creditors to debtors over 10-20 years.
evidence: 17% acute crisis rate, all in non-monetary-sovereign contexts
4/7 historical fiscal dominance episodes produced financial repression
0/5 for default or hyperinflation in own-currency issuers
UK gilt crisis (2022) demonstrated markets can enforce real-rate discipline even on own-currency issuers
Historical sample conflates capital controls/regulated banking with modern capital mobility
Base rate: 4/7 historical episodes produced financial repression
No democracy has sustained financial repression >15 years without external anchor
Stealth repression via Basel III LCR, NAIC RBC, pension mandates may evade democratic constraint
2012-2019 QE characterized as 'accidental' repression for 7 years
Ranking (repression > crisis for reserve-currency issuer) more informative than point probability
open_questions: Is the post-WWII deleveraging toolkit still available given reversed demographics and absence of productivity boom?
Do modern capital mobility conditions invalidate historical analogues?
What form would financial repression take in current institutional context?
Is the 50% probability estimate defensible or does modern capital mobility reduce it?
Can stealth repression through prudential regulation evade both democratic and capital flight constraints?
What is the capacity of crypto/stablecoin as an escape valve from financial repression?
How to detect flow-survey divergence indicating repression onset?
Does modern capital mobility shorten repression sustainability below the 25-35 years required for deleveraging?
